stories.headline: Denver Mayor Johnston launches $100 million jobs initiative targeting 10,000 positions over three years
stories.summary:
Mayor Mike Johnston announced the Denver Jobs Agenda on Thursday, a four-part, $100 million plan designed to create and support 10,000 quality jobs across the city over the next three years. The initiative aims to spur economic growth, support small businesses, and draw new employers to Denver amid persistent remote-work trends and sluggish downtown recovery.

The plan is divided into four programs: Launch Denver, a $6 million fund to help entrepreneurs start businesses; Build Denver, a $10.5 million revolving loan fund for business expansion; Open Denver, a $40 million Downtown Development Authority investment to attract businesses and workers downtown; and Work Denver, a $45 million workforce training and credentialing program.

Johnston made the announcement outside Snooze restaurant on Larimer Street alongside city and Downtown Denver Partnership leaders. David Welsh of the Downtown Denver Partnership noted that each new employer choosing downtown generates ripple effects for restaurants, retailers, hotels and cultural institutions.

Census data show 22.6% of Denver-area workers work from home, well above the national average of 13.3%. City leaders characterized the Jobs Agenda as a direct response to that reality and a tool for competing with other cities for major employer relocations and expansions.

stories.headline: Victor Marx declared winner of Colorado Republican gubernatorial primary after more than a week of vote counting
stories.summary:
Marine veteran Victor Marx has been projected as the winner of the Republican primary for Colorado governor, CNN's Decision Desk announced, more than a week after Election Day. Marx, whose extraordinary personal claims — including that he was forced to kill a man at age 7 — have drawn widespread scrutiny, edged out rivals by winning El Paso County and a collection of rural counties while picking up enough votes on the Front Range.

Marx will face a Democrat in November to succeed two-term Gov. Jared Polis, who is constitutionally barred from seeking a third term. Democrats fielded U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet and state Attorney General Phil Weiser in their own primary.

Marx's unverified biographical claims have overshadowed his policy positions throughout the campaign, and his primary rivals have not endorsed him. Questions about his background are expected to continue through the general election.

Colorado leans Democratic in statewide races, making Marx an underdog in the fall contest regardless of his primary victory.

stories.headline: Update: Aspen Acres fire reaches 96,121 acres at 14% containment; at least 263 structures destroyed
stories.summary:
The Aspen Acres fire in Custer and Pueblo counties has grown to 96,121 acres — roughly the size of the City and County of Denver — and stands at 14% containment as of Thursday, its 11th day of active firefighting operations. At least 263 structures have been destroyed in what is now the seventh-largest fire in Colorado history. More than 1,800 personnel from at least 29 states are working the blaze.

Pueblo County officials said they expect to lift most evacuation orders in the southern part of the county on Friday. Mandatory evacuations remain in effect for communities near the Pueblo-Custer county border, including Beulah, Colorado City and Wetmore, affecting roughly 11,000 people.

Gov. Jared Polis has issued a verbal disaster emergency declaration for the fire, activating the State Emergency Operations Plan and increasing available state resources. A complex incident management team from Alaska has assumed command of the fire.

The Ferris Fire in Dolores and Montezuma counties has separately reached more than 60,000 acres at 19% containment, and the Gold Mountain Fire northeast of Ouray has burned 32,131 acres at 8% containment, with the latter affecting endurance race events across southwestern Colorado.

stories.headline: Denver jury convicts man of first-degree murder in Cheesman Park drug deal shooting; sentencing set for August
stories.summary:
A Denver jury convicted Travis Jamahal Jackson, 41, of first-degree murder in the November 2023 shooting death of Michael Milligan, 28, near Cheesman Park. Jackson also was found guilty of attempted murder, second-degree murder and assault. The two-week trial concluded with a verdict that carries a mandatory sentence of life in prison without the possibility of parole under Colorado law.

According to prosecutors, Milligan and a friend arranged to sell Jackson four pounds of marijuana purchased from a medical dispensary. Instead of paying, Jackson shot Milligan at the meeting location and fled. Detectives tracked Jackson through his phone number and a witness identification.

Jackson is scheduled to be sentenced Aug. 20. His attorney, Anthony Camera of Camera Law in Denver, said the case presented strong grounds for appeal and indicated the defense plans to challenge the verdict.

The conviction marks the resolution of a case that began with Milligan's killing in late 2023 in one of Denver's historically active parks.

stories.headline: Bear-scavenged human remains found near Grand Mesa picnic area; Mesa County investigating death
stories.summary:
Mesa County Sheriff's Office investigators are examining the death of an unidentified person after human remains were discovered near the Wild Rose Picnic Area on the Grand Mesa last Sunday. The remains, found off Land Ends Road in the Grand Mesa, Uncompahgre and Gunnison National Forests, had been scattered and partially consumed by a bear and other wildlife.

The cause and manner of death have not been determined and will be established by the Mesa County Coroner's Office. Colorado Parks and Wildlife said it does not believe there is any public safety threat related to the bear activity, noting that human-bear conflicts in the area are typically tied to human behavior.

Authorities are asking anyone who may have observed something suspicious near the picnic area between June 22 and last Sunday to contact Investigator Jenna Reed at 970-244-3274.

The Grand Mesa is located in western Colorado, roughly 40 miles east of Grand Junction.

stories.headline: DPS Superintendent Marrero fails to advance in Miami-Dade search, reaffirms commitment to Denver
stories.summary:
Denver Public Schools Superintendent Alex Marrero was not selected as one of the six semifinalists for the Miami-Dade County Public Schools superintendent position, ending his candidacy for the Florida role. Marrero issued a statement saying he remains committed to Denver students and families and that his bid did not diminish his focus on DPS.

The news follows a period of uncertainty after Marrero sent a June 8 letter to the DPS board alleging contract violations that could have entitled him to resign with a year's salary and benefits. The letter generated community concern and board discussions about his future with the district.

DPS Board President Xóchitl Gaytán announced a virtual special board meeting has been scheduled for July 27 to address questions raised by Marrero's June memo. Marrero declined an interview on the matter last week and has communicated largely through written statements.

Marrero's statement highlighted academic gains under his tenure, including graduation rates at an all-time high and DPS earning a green rating on Colorado's District Performance Framework for only the second time in district history.

stories.headline: Colorado's summer air quality crisis may be the new normal, health experts warn
stories.summary:
Colorado health and environmental experts say this summer's repeated bouts of hazardous air quality — driven by a combination of wildfire smoke and ground-level ozone — should be treated as a persistent public health emergency rather than a seasonal anomaly. Denver recorded the worst air quality of any U.S. city on both Monday and Tuesday, briefly ranking among the top ten most polluted cities globally, according to the air quality tracking firm IQAir.

The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment has already issued 20 ozone action alerts this summer, compared with 30 for all of last year, with the ozone season running through August 31. Dr. David Beuther, a pulmonologist at National Jewish Health, said patients who once found relief in summer are now struggling with smoke-related breathing problems.

CSU epidemiologist Sheryl Magzamen, who studies the health effects of wildfire smoke, described the situation as a "silent epidemic." Fine particles in smoke, known as PM 2.5, can penetrate deep into the lungs, cause inflammation, and potentially contribute to cognitive changes, research has shown.

Beuther urged Coloradans to create an emergency preparedness plan for bad air quality — including identifying indoor refuges with clean air and stocking N95 masks — the same way they would plan for floods or fires. High temperatures forecast for the weekend are expected to bring another round of elevated ozone.

stories.headline: RTD weighing sweeping network overhaul as agency faces $215 million deficit and 20% service cut scenario
stories.summary:
The Regional Transportation District is in the early stages of a broad reassessment of its transit network, including a planning scenario that assumes a 20% reduction in service, as the agency confronts a projected $215 million budget deficit. An RTD planner told the Denver Gazette the analysis starts from a blank map, with "pretty much everything on the table."

At the center of the review is a fundamental question: whether RTD should concentrate service on routes with the highest ridership or maintain coverage across low-density areas where few riders use the system. Fares currently cover only 14% of RTD's operating costs, with the remainder funded largely through regional sales-tax revenue.

The Gazette editorial board cited the reassessment as a potential turning point for an agency that has long struggled with low ridership on many routes, particularly in far-flung suburbs. Recent state legislation reconfiguring RTD's elected board was characterized as unlikely to resolve the agency's underlying structural problems.

The outcome of RTD's review could set a precedent for other Front Range transit systems facing similar pressures, including Mountain Metropolitan Transit in Colorado Springs.

stories.headline: Colorado wildfires force cancellation of Ouray 100, Silver Rush events; Hardrock 100 proceeds after close call
stories.summary:
Several marquee Colorado endurance events have been canceled or postponed this summer due to wildfires and smoke, while the iconic Hardrock 100 ultramarathon in Silverton cleared its final air quality hurdle Thursday morning and will proceed as scheduled Saturday. About 150 runners are set to tackle the 100-mile course climbing more than 33,000 feet over 13 mountain passes.

The Ouray 100 and Leadville's Silver Rush mountain bike and ultramarathons were called off due to the Gold Mountain Fire, which has burned more than 32,000 acres northeast of Ouray at 8% containment. The Leadville Trail 100 in August remains in limbo. Race directors cited course damage from the fire and the health risk of extreme respiratory exertion during heavy smoke events.

Hardrock organizers used an air quality decision matrix tied to EPA monitoring stations in Silverton, Ouray and Telluride, setting a threshold of AQI 150 as an automatic cancellation trigger. Readings came in below that level Thursday morning. Nine separate government agencies — including four counties, three municipalities, the U.S. Forest Service and the Bureau of Land Management — were consulted before the final go-ahead was given.

The divergent outcomes highlight the difficult calculations race directors across Colorado face as wildfire seasons lengthen and intensify, with economic impacts on host communities adding pressure to proceed even under uncertain conditions.

stories.headline: CU Boulder study concludes moose are native to Colorado, not invasive species introduced in 1978
stories.summary:
A new study led by University of Colorado Boulder archaeologists concludes that moose were a part of Colorado's ancient fauna long before the state's 1978 wildlife introduction program, potentially reframing ongoing debates about managing the animals in places like Rocky Mountain National Park. The research, published in the Journal of Biogeography, combines bone records, tribal oral traditions, historical photographs, and 19th- and 20th-century journals.

Lead author William Taylor, associate professor and archaeology curator at CU Boulder, said the evidence supports moose having established populations in the southern Rockies during the 19th and 20th centuries and likely as far back as the Early Holocene when glaciers retreated northward. The study explicitly states: "Moose were a part of Colorado's ancient fauna, and are not invasive."

The findings carry practical implications for land management. Rocky Mountain National Park has considered fencing or even culling moose populations because the animals, along with elk, are consuming willows that beavers need to build dams, reducing wetland habitat and ecological diversity. If moose are recognized as native rather than introduced species, managers may weigh that differently in future decisions.

Colorado Parks and Wildlife introduced 24 moose from Wyoming and Utah in 1978. The population has since grown substantially, with concentrations in areas including North Park and RMNP.

stories.headline: Broncos defensive tackle Zach Allen named No. 73 on NFL Top 100 list, eyes all-time sack record
stories.summary:
Denver Broncos defensive tackle Zach Allen was named the 73rd-best player in the NFL by the league's annual Top 100 countdown on Thursday, becoming the first Broncos player to appear on the 2026 list. Allen led the NFL with 47 quarterback hits in 2025, recorded seven sacks and an 11.5% pressure rate, and was named a first-team Associated Press All-Pro for the first time in his career.

Allen, 28 and entering his fourth season with Denver, said the Broncos defense "definitely expects" to break the NFL single-season sack record this year. The team set a franchise record with 63 sacks last season, falling five short of the all-time mark set by the 1984 Chicago Bears.

Allen signed a four-year, $102 million extension during training camp last summer. Teammates praised his unorthodox pass-rush technique, with fellow All-Pro cornerback Patrick Surtain II describing his moves as "like watching ballet on grass."

Additional Denver players are expected to appear on the Top 100 list as the countdown continues toward No. 1.

stories.headline: Rockies drop series finale to Dodgers 4-3 on Mookie Betts eighth-inning single; open series against Giants
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies lost 4-3 to the Los Angeles Dodgers on Wednesday night when Mookie Betts singled home the go-ahead run in the eighth inning off reliever Antonio Senzatela, who fell to 8-1 on the season. The Dodgers won two of three in the series, denying Colorado its first series win at Dodger Stadium since August 2021.

Rookie starter Gabriel Hughes, 24, allowed three runs in a rocky first inning but then retired 15 consecutive batters before giving way to the bullpen. Hughes struck out Shohei Ohtani three times during his first major league start. The Rockies tied the game at three on solo home runs by Kyle Karros and Edouard Julien off Dodgers starter Roki Sasaki.

Colorado loaded the bases in the eighth against the Dodgers bullpen but could not capitalize, with Edgardo Henriquez retiring Kyle Karros on a fly ball for the final out. Tanner Scott closed out the ninth for his 13th save.

The Rockies travel to San Francisco to open a series against the Giants, with Ryan Feltner starting Thursday against Robbie Ray.

joke.joke: You know what tells you that you're out of shape? When you consider sweating a workout.

weekendEvents.title: Denver French Fest
weekendEvents.description: A free three-day celebration of French food and culture featuring music, dance, a fashion show, and more. Paid VIP wine and cuisine experiences are also available.

weekendEvents.title: Pokémon GO Fest 10th Anniversary
weekendEvents.description: Trainers gather at Utah Park in Aurora to celebrate the 10th anniversary of Pokémon GO with raids, gym battles, and more. The event is free to attend.
weekendEvents.location: Utah Park, 1800 S. Peoria St., Aurora

weekendEvents.title: Market at the Center Ducky Derby
weekendEvents.description: Hundreds of rubber ducks race down the South Platte River in a community competition benefitting the GES Community Investment Fund. Free to attend; duck sponsorships available at various price points.
weekendEvents.location: Riverfront Open Space at the National Western Center, Denver

weekendEvents.title: After Dark 90s-00s R&B Dance Party
weekendEvents.description: A monthly dance party at Punch Bowl Social spinning R&B throwbacks from the 1990s and 2000s. Admission is free; drinks available for purchase.

weekendEvents.title: Sincere Engineer
weekendEvents.description: Live concert at the Marquis Theater featuring Sincere Engineer.

weekendEvents.title: Chicken Wire Empire
weekendEvents.description: Live performance by Chicken Wire Empire at Lone Tree Arts Center.
weekendEvents.location: Lone Tree Arts Center, Lone Tree

weekendEvents.title: Red Rocks Weekend: The Avett Brothers with The Mountain Goats
weekendEvents.description: One of America's favorite folk-rock bands opens a special two-night Red Rocks run joined by indie folk legends The Mountain Goats, featuring heartfelt storytelling and singalongs.

weekendEvents.title: Red Rocks: Graham Nash and The Avett Brothers
weekendEvents.description: The weekend's Red Rocks run concludes with a collaboration featuring Rock and Roll Hall of Famer Graham Nash alongside The Avett Brothers.

weekendEvents.title: Asleep At The Wheel at Red Rocks
weekendEvents.description: Legendary country swing pioneers Asleep At The Wheel perform beneath the stars at Red Rocks on the second night of the weekend run.

weekendEvents.title: Shakedown Street: A Tribute to The Grateful Dead
weekendEvents.description: A Grateful Dead tribute performance at Swallow Hill Music Hall.

topStory.headline: U.S. Strikes Hit 90 Targets in Iran; Tehran Retaliates Against Gulf Allies as Ceasefire Teeters
topStory.summary:
The United States military carried out airstrikes against approximately 90 Iranian military targets on Wednesday, hitting air defense systems, coastal surveillance assets, missile and drone storage sites, naval capabilities and military logistics infrastructure along Iran's coastline. Iran retaliated with waves of strikes against U.S.-allied nations in the region, triggering air raid sirens in Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar and later Jordan. U.S. Central Command released footage appearing to show strikes on an airport runway and missile launchers, and said Thursday it had concluded its latest round of attacks.

Iran's Foreign Ministry condemned the U.S. strikes as a "grave war crime," accusing Washington of targeting civilian infrastructure including two railway bridges in Golestan province, on the route to Mashhad where Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was being buried Thursday. The Iranian Health Ministry reported at least 14 people killed and 78 wounded across two days of attacks. A U.S. official denied striking the Bushehr nuclear power plant after an Iranian local official claimed an airstrike hit its perimeter, and also denied that further U.S. offensive strikes had been carried out on Thursday, stating the military had only been shooting down incoming Iranian projectiles. Falling debris from interceptions caused some damage, including to a non-U.S. building near the U.S. Navy's 5th Fleet headquarters in Bahrain.

The escalation unfolded as thousands of mourners in black filled the streets of Mashhad for the final day of funeral ceremonies for Khamenei, whose coffin was carried to the Imam Reza shrine for burial. His brother Mostafa Khamenei led prayers over the coffin. Iran's new Supreme Leader is Mojtaba Khamenei, the late leader's son.

The fighting represents a sharp escalation of a conflict launched by the United States and Israel in February 2026. The two sides had reached an interim ceasefire agreement, but repeated exchanges of fire — including a similar cycle in late June triggered by an Iranian attack on a commercial ship in the Strait of Hormuz — have repeatedly pushed the deal to the brink. President Trump said he considered the ceasefire effectively over following Iranian attacks on Strait of Hormuz shipping that preceded the latest U.S. strikes.

The back-and-forth raises serious doubts about whether Washington and Tehran remain committed to negotiating a final agreement to end the war. With the ceasefire under severe strain, U.S. allies across the Gulf on heightened alert and Iran's new leadership just assuming power, diplomats and analysts face uncertainty over whether any framework for de-escalation remains viable in the near term.
